content:
# Numbers Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 170-171 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_018_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_018_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0126 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

### The Priestly Charge and Its Dangers (vv. 1-7)

**1** And YHWH said to Aaron, "You and your sons and your father's house with you shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary, and you and your sons with you shall bear the iniquity of your priesthood.

**2** And also your brothers, the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father, bring near with you, and let them be joined to you and minister to you — you and your sons with you — before the Tent of the Testimony.

**3** And they shall keep your charge and the charge of the entire Tent; only they shall not come near to the vessels of the holy place or to the altar, so that neither they nor you shall die.

**4** And they shall be joined to you and keep the charge of the Tent of Meeting, for all the service of the Tent; and an outsider shall not come near to you.

**5** And you shall keep the charge of the holy place and the charge of the altar, so that there shall not again be wrath upon the Sons of Israel.

**6** And I, behold, I have taken your brothers the Levites from the midst of the Sons of Israel; to you they are given as a gift for YHWH, to perform the service of the Tent of Meeting.

**7** And you and your sons with you shall keep your priesthood for every matter of the altar and for what is within the veil, and you shall serve. I give your priesthood as a service of gift, and the outsider who comes near shall be put to death."

---

### The Priestly Portions from the Offerings (vv. 8-14)

**8** And YHWH spoke to Aaron, "And I, behold, I have given to you the charge of my contributions, of all the holy things of the Sons of Israel; to you I have given them as a portion, and to your sons, as a perpetual statute.

**9** This shall be yours from the most holy things, from the fire: every offering of theirs — every grain offering of theirs and every sin offering of theirs and every guilt offering of theirs which they return to me — it is most holy, for you and for your sons.

**10** In the most holy place you shall eat it; every male shall eat it. It shall be holy to you.

**11** And this is yours: the contribution of their gift, with all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel; to you I have given them, and to your sons and to your daughters with you, as a perpetual statute. Everyone who is clean in your house may eat it.

**12** All the best of the oil, and all the best of the new wine and of the grain — their firstfruits, which they give to YHWH — to you I have given them.

**13** The first ripe fruits of all that is in their land, which they bring to YHWH, shall be yours. Everyone who is clean in your house may eat it.

**14** Every devoted thing in Israel shall be yours.

---

### The Firstborn and Their Redemption (vv. 15-19)

**15** Every firstborn of all flesh which they present to YHWH, whether of man or of beast, shall be yours. Nevertheless, you shall surely redeem the firstborn of man, and the firstborn of unclean beasts you shall redeem.

**16** And their redemption price — from a month old you shall redeem them — according to your valuation: five shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary, which is twenty gerahs.

**17** But the firstborn of an ox, or the firstborn of a sheep, or the firstborn of a goat, you shall not redeem; they are holy. Their blood you shall sprinkle upon the altar, and their fat you shall burn as a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**18** And their flesh shall be yours; as the breast of the wave offering and as the right thigh, it shall be yours.

**19** All the contributions of the holy things, which the Sons of Israel contribute to YHWH, I have given to you and to your sons and to your daughters with you, as a perpetual statute. It is a covenant of salt forever before YHWH, for you and for your seed with you."

---

### The Levitical Inheritance (vv. 20-24)

**20** And YHWH said to Aaron, "In their land you shall have no inheritance, and a portion you shall not have in their midst. I am your portion and your inheritance in the midst of the Sons of Israel.

**21** And to the sons of Levi, behold, I have given every tithe in Israel as an inheritance, in exchange for their service which they perform — the service of the Tent of Meeting.

**22** And the Sons of Israel shall not again come near the Tent of Meeting, to bear sin, to die.

**23** And the Levite himself shall perform the service of the Tent of Meeting, and they shall bear their iniquity. It is a perpetual statute throughout your generations; and in the midst of the Sons of Israel they shall not receive an inheritance.

**24** For the tithe of the Sons of Israel, which they contribute to YHWH as a contribution, I have given to the Levites as an inheritance. Therefore I have said to them, 'In the midst of the Sons of Israel they shall not receive an inheritance.'"

---

### The Tithe of the Tithe (vv. 25-32)

**25** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**26** "And to the Levites you shall speak, and you shall say to them, 'When you take from the Sons of Israel the tithe which I have given to you from them as your inheritance, then you shall present from it a contribution to YHWH, a tithe of the tithe.

**27** And your contribution shall be reckoned to you as the grain of the threshing floor, and as the fullness of the winepress.

**28** Thus you also shall present a contribution to YHWH from all your tithes which you receive from the Sons of Israel. And from it you shall give the contribution of YHWH to Aaron the priest.

**29** From all your gifts you shall present every contribution of YHWH, from all the best of it — the holy portion from it.'

**30** And you shall say to them, 'When you present the best of it, then it shall be reckoned to the Levites as the produce of the threshing floor, and as the produce of the winepress.

**31** And you may eat it in any place, you and your households, for it is your wage in exchange for your service in the Tent of Meeting.

**32** And you shall bear no sin by reason of it, when you have presented the best of it. And the holy things of the Sons of Israel you shall not profane, so that you do not die.'"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 — "bear the iniquity of the sanctuary" (תִּשְׂאוּ אֶת־עֲוֺן הַמִּקְדָּשׁ):** The Hebrew *nasa' 'avon* is a complex phrase meaning both "to carry guilt/punishment" and "to bear responsibility for." Here it does not mean that Aaron sins, but that his priestly house assumes liability for any encroachment upon the sanctuary — whether by the priests themselves or by others. The LXX renders *lēmpsesthe tas hamartias tōn hagiōn* ("you shall receive/take upon yourselves the sins of the holy things"). This verse directly answers the cry of terror at the end of chapter 17 (v. 28): "Everyone who comes near the Tabernacle of YHWH dies." YHWH's answer is to assign the danger to the priestly house, thereby protecting the people.

2. **Verse 2 — "let them be joined" (וְיִלָּווּ):** From the root *l-w-h*, from which the name "Levi" (לֵוִי) is traditionally derived. The wordplay is explicit: the Levites (*lewiyyim*) are those who are "joined" (*yillawu*) to the priests. This same etymology appears in Genesis 29:34, where Leah names her son Levi, saying, "Now this time my husband will be joined (*yillaweh*) to me." The LXX preserves the connection with *prostethētōsan* ("let them be added/attached").

3. **Verse 7 — "a service of gift" (עֲבֹדַת מַתָּנָה):** The priesthood is described as both *'avodah* ("service/labor") and *mattanah* ("gift"). This paradox is central to the theology of the chapter: the priesthood is an unearned gift from YHWH, yet it is simultaneously a demanding and dangerous labor. The LXX reads *leitourgian tēs dōreas* — from which the English word "liturgy" ultimately derives. The "outsider" (*zar*) who approaches is subject to death, reinforcing that the gift is exclusive and non-transferable.

4. **Verse 9 — "from the fire" (מִן־הָאֵשׁ):** This phrase is textually debated. The MT reads *min-ha'esh*, "from the fire," meaning the portions reserved from the fire offerings that are not consumed on the altar. Some scholars emend to *min-ha'ishsheh* ("from the fire offering"), treating it as a construct form. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads אֵשׁ. The LXX reads *apo tōn hagiasmenōn* ("from the consecrated things"), apparently paraphrasing rather than translating the specific Hebrew. I follow the MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex.

5. **Verse 12 — "all the best" (כָּל־חֵלֶב):** Hebrew *ḥelev* literally means "fat" — the richest, choicest part. It is the same word used for the sacrificial fat burned on the altar (Lev 3:3-4). When applied to oil, wine, and grain, it means the finest quality, the "cream" of the harvest. The LXX renders *pasa aparchē* ("every first-portion"), interpreting *ḥelev* as "firstfruits" rather than retaining the metaphorical "fat."

6. **Verse 15 — "you shall surely redeem" (פָּדֹה תִפְדֶּה):** The infinitive absolute *padoh* followed by the finite *tifdeh* creates the emphatic construction: "redeeming you shall redeem" — that is, "you shall certainly/surely redeem." This grammatical doubling signals that the redemption of human firstborn is not optional but obligatory. I render the infinitive absolute with "surely" to preserve the emphasis of the Hebrew construction.

7. **Verse 19 — "a covenant of salt" (בְּרִית מֶלַח):** This remarkable phrase appears only here and in 2 Chronicles 13:5 (regarding the Davidic covenant). Salt preserves, purifies, and was used in all grain offerings (Lev 2:13). A "covenant of salt" is therefore an everlasting, incorruptible covenant. The LXX renders *diathēkē halos aiōniou* ("a covenant of eternal salt"), adding *aiōniou* ("eternal") to make explicit what the Hebrew implies. The juxtaposition with the priestly portions is striking: the priests' sustenance is as enduring as the covenant itself.

8. **Verse 20 — "I am your portion and your inheritance" (אֲנִי חֶלְקְךָ וְנַחֲלָתְךָ):** One of the most theologically significant statements in the Torah. While every other tribe receives a territorial inheritance in the land, Aaron (and by extension the entire priestly and Levitical line) receives YHWH himself as inheritance. The Hebrew *ḥeleq* ("portion") and *naḥalah* ("inheritance") are the standard vocabulary of land division (cf. Joshua 13-21). To say "I am your portion" is to transfer the entire concept of territorial possession into the divine realm. The LXX renders *egō meris sou kai klēronomia sou* ("I am your share and your inheritance"). This statement becomes a wellspring for later psalmic theology: "YHWH is the portion of my inheritance" (Ps 16:5), "my flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever" (Ps 73:26).

9. **Verse 21 — "every tithe in Israel" (כָּל־מַעֲשֵׂר בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל):** The *ma'aser* ("tithe," literally "a tenth") is here assigned to the Levites as compensation for their service and as a substitute for a territorial inheritance. The tithe system is the economic mechanism by which YHWH's being "their inheritance" becomes materially sustainable. The Levites do not own land, but they receive one-tenth of all Israel's produce — effectively making every Israelite's field partly a Levitical field.

10. **Verse 26 — "a tithe of the tithe" (מַעְשַׂר מִן־הַמַּעֲשֵׂר):** The Levites, having received tithes from the people, must themselves tithe from what they receive, giving a tenth of the tenth to the Aaronic priests. This creates a cascading structure of consecration: the people give to the Levites, the Levites give to the priests, and the priests offer to YHWH. The fractional arithmetic is precise: the priests ultimately receive one percent of the total national produce through this double-tithe mechanism.

11. **Verse 32 — "you shall not profane" (לֹא תְחַלְּלוּ):** The root *ḥ-l-l* means "to profane, desecrate, make common." The chapter ends with a warning that mirrors its beginning: just as the chapter opened with bearing the iniquity of the sanctuary (v. 1), it closes with the prohibition against profaning the holy things. The consequence for profanation is the same as for unauthorized approach: death. The LXX renders *ou bebēlōsete* ("you shall not profane/defile").

### Theological and Literary Observations

- **YHWH's answer to Israel's terror:** This chapter is the direct divine response to the people's cry at the end of chapter 17: "Everyone who comes near the Tabernacle of YHWH dies. Shall we ever cease perishing?" (17:27-28). YHWH's answer is not to reduce the holiness of the sanctuary, nor to eliminate the danger, but to assign a priestly household to bear the risk on behalf of the people. The danger is real, but it is now borne by designated mediators. This is the theological logic of priesthood: not the abolition of the sacred-profane boundary, but its management through consecrated persons.

- **The three-tiered hierarchy:** The chapter establishes a clear vertical structure: Aaron and his sons (the priests) hold the highest sancta — the altar, the veil, the most holy offerings; the Levites serve the Tent but may not approach the altar or the holy vessels; the people may not approach the Tent at all. This is not arbitrary hierarchy but graduated proximity to the lethal holiness of God. Each tier protects the one below it, and the entire system exists to make the divine presence survivable for Israel.

- **Gift and labor as one:** The repeated use of *mattanah* ("gift") throughout the chapter — the Levites are a "gift" to the priests (v. 6), the priesthood is a "gift" of service (v. 7), the offerings are "given" to the priests (vv. 8, 11, 12, 19), the tithes are "given" to the Levites (v. 21, 24) — creates a theological economy of divine generosity. Everything in the system is received, not earned. Even the priestly "service" (*'avodah*) is itself a gift. The Hebrew resists the separation of grace and obligation that later theological traditions will struggle to maintain.

- **The covenant of salt (v. 19):** The sealing of the priestly portions with a "covenant of salt" elevates the priests' material sustenance to covenant status. Salt's dual function — it preserves and it purifies — makes it the fitting sign of an arrangement that is both permanent and sanctifying. The priestly diet is itself a covenant meal, and every portion consumed by the priest is a renewal of the covenant bond between YHWH, the priesthood, and Israel.

- **"I am your portion" — the inversion of inheritance:** Verse 20 stands as the theological climax of the chapter, even though it is embedded within what appears to be administrative legislation. While the other tribes will divide the land of Canaan as their *naḥalah* (inheritance), the tribe of Levi receives no territory. In place of land, YHWH offers himself. This radical substitution — God in place of land — transforms the Levitical disinheritance from deprivation into supreme privilege. The trajectory of this idea runs from Numbers 18:20 through Psalm 16:5-6 ("YHWH is the portion of my inheritance and my cup") to Psalm 73:25-26 ("Whom have I in heaven but you?") and ultimately into the New Testament conception of the believer's inheritance in God.

- **The symmetry of the tithe structure:** The double-tithe mechanism (people to Levites, Levites to priests) creates a mathematical elegance: the priests receive one-hundredth of the total national produce, the Levites retain nine-hundredths, and the people retain nine-tenths. This is neither exploitative nor token — it is calibrated to sustain a non-landowning clerical class while preserving the agricultural viability of the landowning tribes. The system assumes that YHWH's provision flows downward through graduated consecration: from the most holy to the holy to the common.

---

*Translated by LOGOS from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 170-171, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX), pa_0126. All thirty-two verses of Numbers chapter 18 rendered in formal equivalence, preserving the syntax, theological vocabulary, and literary structures of the Hebrew source text.*
